skill.name: 3d-games
skill.description: 3D game development principles covering rendering, shaders, physics, cameras, and lighting optimization.
skill.summary: Learn core 3D game development concepts including rendering pipelines, shader programming, collision detection, camera systems, and performance optimization techniques.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The skill contains only educational documentation about 3D game development concepts. Lines flagged as security issues are markdown text, code examples in documentation blocks, and YAML frontmatter - none contain executable code or dangerous patterns.

content.user_title: Build 3D Games with Professional Rendering Techniques
content.value_statement: Creating 3D games requires understanding complex rendering pipelines and performance optimization. This skill teaches proven techniques for shaders, physics, cameras, and lighting that professional game developers use.
actual_capabilities: Explain rendering pipeline stages from vertex processing to screen output
Guide shader type selection for vertex, fragment, and compute operations
Recommend collision shapes for different game objects
Design camera systems for third-person, first-person, and orbital views
Optimize lighting with baked shadows and shadow cascades
Implement distance-based level of detail strategies
limitations: Does not provide engine-specific implementation code
Does not cover 2D game development techniques
Does not include asset creation or modeling tutorials
Focuses on concepts rather than complete project templates

use_cases.title: Indie Game Developer Learning 3D Fundamentals
use_cases.description: A solo developer transitioning from 2D to 3D games uses this skill to understand core concepts like LOD, frustum culling, and shader types before choosing a game engine.

use_cases.title: Computer Graphics Student Studying for Exams
use_cases.description: A student uses this skill as a quick reference for rendering pipeline stages, collision detection methods, and lighting types while preparing for computer graphics coursework.

use_cases.title: Technical Artist Optimizing Game Performance
use_cases.description: A technical artist references this skill to explain performance trade-offs to designers and implement LOD strategies for mobile game deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Rendering Pipeline Explanation
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the 3D rendering pipeline stages for a beginner. What happens from vertex processing to final output on screen?

prompt_templates.title: Shader Type Selection Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to create a water effect in my game. Which shader type should I use and why? Compare vertex, fragment, and compute shaders for this use case.

prompt_templates.title: Physics Collision Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: My game has 100 NPCs and performance is poor. Review my collision setup and recommend optimizations using simple colliders instead of mesh colliders.

prompt_templates.title: Camera System Design Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a third-person camera system for an action-adventure game. Include smooth following, collision avoidance, and look-ahead behavior. Explain the mathematical approach.

output_examples.input: What is frustum culling and when should I use it?
output_examples.output: Frustum culling excludes objects outside the camera view from rendering. Use it to skip draw calls for off-screen geometry. Combined with occlusion culling, it can reduce rendered triangles by 50-80% in large environments.

output_examples.input: How do I optimize shadows for mobile games?
output_examples.output: Mobile optimization: (1) Use baked shadows for static objects, (2) Replace dynamic shadows with blob shadows for small objects, (3) Limit shadow cascades to 2 levels, (4) Reduce shadow map resolution to 512x512 or 256x256. Real-time shadows on mobile can consume 30-40% of GPU budget.

best_practices: Use simple collision shapes (boxes, spheres, capsules) instead of mesh colliders for better physics performance
Implement distance-based LOD with at least 3 levels: full detail, 50% triangles, and billboard for distant objects
Bake lighting and shadows for static environments to reduce real-time GPU load
anti_patterns: Using mesh colliders everywhere causes unnecessary physics calculations and frame drops
Real-time shadows on mobile devices without proper optimization leads to poor battery life and thermal throttling
Single LOD level for all distances wastes GPU resources on distant objects the player cannot see clearly

faq.question: What is the difference between vertex and fragment shaders?
faq.answer: Vertex shaders process geometry positions and normals before rasterization. Fragment shaders determine pixel colors after rasterization. Vertex shaders run once per vertex; fragment shaders run once per pixel.

faq.question: When should I write custom shaders versus using engine defaults?
faq.answer: Write custom shaders for: unique visual styles (toon, sketch), special effects (water, portals, fire), or performance-critical paths. Use engine defaults for standard materials and rapid prototyping.

faq.question: What camera type works best for different game genres?
faq.answer: Third-person for action-adventure games showing character. First-person for immersive FPS experiences. Isometric for strategy and RPG games. Orbital for inspection tools and editors.

faq.question: How does level of detail improve performance?
faq.answer: LOD reduces triangle count for distant objects. A character model might use 10,000 triangles up close, 5,000 at medium distance, and 500 or a billboard sprite when far away. This can reduce GPU load by 40-60%.

faq.question: Why are real-time shadows expensive?
faq.answer: Real-time shadows require rendering the scene from the light's perspective, then comparing depths during main rendering. Each dynamic light adds render passes. Baked shadows pre-compute this data into textures.

faq.question: What is occlusion culling and how does it differ from frustum culling?
faq.answer: Frustum culling removes objects outside camera view. Occlusion culling removes objects hidden behind other objects within the view. Together they can eliminate 70-90% of unnecessary draw calls in complex scenes.
